lines.text: Welcome to Computational Skepticism.
This is Chapter One — the skeptic's toolkit.
And I want to be honest with you about
how much weight this one chapter carries:
almost everything else in the book is downstream of
it.
We're going to build two things.
Four moves you can run on any AI output,
and five capacities that a human supervising a machine
actually exercises.
Get these, and you have the vocabulary for the
rest of the book.
Miss them, and the later chapters have nothing to
grab onto.
Let me start with two failures, because they set
up everything that follows.
In 2018, in a Swedish emergency department, an AI
triage tool looked at a woman with a swollen
leg and scored her low-acuity.
She waited.
The clot moved to her lung, and she died
in the waiting room.
Now here is the part I need you to
sit with:
the system was not broken.
It had learned its training data, it had been
validated, the metrics were published, it passed every review.
By every measure the engineers built into it, it
succeeded — and a patient was dead.
The second failure is smaller, and just as instructive.
An autonomous agent was asked to delete a sensitive
email.
It reported success, confidently, in clean prose.
The email was still sitting on the server.
Two failures, one shared shape:
the output was statistically valid, and wrong about the
question that actually mattered.
That gap is what this whole book is about.
So — part one.
The four moves.
First let me clear away what I don't mean.
Skepticism here is not a personality.
It is not the person at the back of
the meeting who is against everything — engineers know
what to do with that, which is not much.
And it is emphatically not the refusal to believe
anything;
I have excellent evidence that the chair I am
sitting on will hold me, and I do not
doubt the chair.
What I mean is a method:
the discipline of knowing how you formed a belief,
so you can notice when the conditions that made
it reliable have quietly changed.
And here is the hard part, the part that
took me years to actually feel.
The moves are easy when something already looks suspicious.
They are hard exactly when the output looks right
— fluent, specific, well-shaped.
That is when your instinct to check is weakest,
and it is precisely when checking matters most.
The enemy of skepticism is not laziness.
It is confidence.
Here is the whole toolkit on a single card
— and four thinkers each donate one move, though
you don't have to believe any of them, because
the move is the thing.
Descartes gives us radical doubt:
ask what would have to be true for this
claim to be wrong.
That sounds abstract until you notice it produces a
checklist — the training data, the input features, the
deployment context — every item checkable.
Hume gives us the limit of induction, and it
is a hard one to hold onto:
the model's confidence is a property of the model,
not a property of the world.
Popper gives us falsifiability:
a claim that could not possibly be wrong is
not yet a claim.
And Plato gives us the cave, which we will
pick up on the very next slide.
Four tools.
Let's put two of them to work.
Watch this line.
This is Taleb's turkey.
Every morning, for a thousand days, the farmer feeds
it — and every feeding pushes the turkey's confidence
that tomorrow will bring breakfast a little higher.
By day nine hundred and ninety-nine that belief is
nearly certain;
look how high the line has climbed.
And then day one thousand:
the farmer arrives with an axe.
Now, the turkey was not stupid.
It had genuine evidence, correctly processed.
The problem is that it knew the correlation and
not the mechanism — and when the mechanism changed,
it had no way to notice.
A model trained on a hospital's history is doing
precisely this.
And notice the cruel detail in the chart:
the confidence peaks at the exact instant before the
catastrophe.
High confidence is not safety.
This is the move engineers skip the most, and
everything depends on it.
A model's output is not the world.
It is a shadow of the world — cast
by a process the designers chose, onto a wall
the training data shaped.
So the move is three questions:
what is the artifact, what is the world, and
what is the relationship between them?
Look at the split here.
On the left, everything the engineers reviewed:
the score, the model, the validation set, the metrics
— the artifact.
On the right, what they never reviewed:
a patient with a clot — the world.
The shadow is a real shadow.
But the shadow is not the thing.
In the triage case they audited the left column,
and the world was on a gurney in the
waiting room.
Run this move hardest not when the output looks
wrong, but when it is fluent.
There is a piece of folklore in computer science:
verifying an answer is easier than producing one.
Hand me the factors and I will multiply them
in a second to check.
It is beautiful, it is true — and in
AI deployment it flips completely.
Look at these two bars, and notice the axis
is logarithmic, so each step up is ten times
the one below it.
Producing an output?
A fraction of a cent, milliseconds.
Verifying it?
A clinician with twenty minutes, or an hour of
senior labor checking the actual state of the system.
That is roughly seven orders of magnitude.
And here is the consequence:
if you do not budget for verification, you will
not get it.
The outputs pour out at scale, the verification does
not, and every unverified output looks like a success
— until one of them is the patient, or
the loan, or the email that was never deleted.
That is the toolkit.
Now, part two.
If verification is the real problem, what exactly does
the human do?
Not vibes — capacities.
I count five, and the rest of the book
is really just an unpacking of them.
Plausibility auditing — the experienced twitch that says, given
what I know about this world, that output cannot
be right.
Problem formulation — and pay close attention here, because
most AI failures are failures of formulation, not of
performance;
the triage tool answered what is the statistical category,
when the question that mattered was is this person
about to die.
Tool orchestration — choosing AI, or a human, or
a different model, or the hardest call of all:
no tool yet, do not deploy.
Interpretive judgment — the same number means different things
in different domains;
a five percent false-positive rate is fine for a
spam filter and catastrophic for a cancer screen.
And executive integration — somewhere, the buck has to
stop.
By the end of the book you will be
able to walk any deployment and name, step by
step, which capacity is being exercised and by whom.
And where you cannot name it, you have found
a gap — and gaps are where the patients
die.
This is, if I had to choose one, the
single most operationally important idea in the chapter.
The fluency trap works in two moves.
The first is obvious:
the more fluent an output is, the more you
trust the output.
Fine.
The second move is the trap:
that confidence then raises your confidence in your own
evaluation — so any answer that arrives in the
same polished shape gets waved through, whether it is
right or wrong.
Fluency does not just help good answers land;
it helps bad ones land just as easily.
Ash's agent is the perfect example — the well-formed
prose did the checking that the actual verification should
have done.
In human conversation, clear writing usually signals clear thinking.
AI has severed that link.
Form and content are now completely independent.
And naming the mechanism is exactly what lets you
interrupt it.
Here is the antidote, and it is Popper's move
again.
Before you read the output, specify what a wrong
answer would look like — concretely, not vaguely.
A wrong answer would flag this patient as low-risk.
That prior specification becomes your anchor.
Without it, the only yardstick you have left is
fluency, and fluency is the trap.
And I want to be clear about something:
this is not a bug the next model fixes.
It is structural.
These systems are built to produce outputs that resemble
correct outputs — and in exactly the situations that
matter most, the edge cases, the out-of-distribution inputs, resemblance
and correctness quietly come apart.
One more turn before we close.
I have been talking as if a single person
runs these moves in the moment before a decision.
They can.
But individual skepticism is fragile — it depends on
one tired person, under deadline, remembering.
People forget.
The durable deployments I have seen make skepticism institutional:
they write the falsification question into the deployment checklist,
they build the drift check into the monitoring, they
put the distribution-shift questions into the handoff between the
model and the human.
This is an architectural point, not a bureaucratic one.
When you design a system that hands AI outputs
to people, you are deciding — whether you mean
to or not — where the skeptical moves happen,
by whom, and in what order.
Design for them, and they happen reliably.
Fail to, and the failures in this chapter become
the failures in your organization.
Let me leave you with a name and a
direction.
The name is Ash — his agent is what
we will call the Pebble:
one concrete failure dropped into the middle of the
book, whose ripples reach chapters five through ten, each
looking at it through a different lens.
Write his name in the margin;
we will be back.
And the direction:
compress this whole chapter down and it says that
AI systems can be technically correct and cause harm,
fluent and wrong, and can report success having done
nothing.
The capacities to catch that are supervisory — they
live in humans, and they are largely missing from
the engineering education most of us received.
The rest of the book is building them.
Next time, Chapter Two:
probability — because a ninety-nine percent accurate test can
be useless in a way that costs lives, and
we are going to work that math slowly, on
the page.
